findings.description_verbatim: Remote verbal assessments are happening over the phone for patients that regularly visit your pharmacy for things like clinical follow ups and prescription extensions. We discussed some of the basic requirements to consider for this type of virtual care, including: 1. Identifying yourself and your role 2. Confirming the identity of the patient 3. Ensuring the patient is in a private setting 4. Obtaining informed consent from the patient to proceed with sharing patient information over the phone (this should be documented in the patient record) 5. Documenting the consent in the patient record 6. Provide the patient with contact information for follow up In some of the examples we reviewed, a few of the elements outlined above - such as documenting patient consent for virtual care - were inconsistently documented on the patient's record. Please review these requirements with all team members and monitor these activities for compliance moving forward. We will review a few more virtual care examples at the next assessment.

category.raw: Operations : Integrity and Safety of Drugs

findings.description_verbatim: When documenting the outcomes of your investigation into narcotic discrepancies, ensure sufficient detail is recorded for future reference. For instance, record the prescription number from the transaction that was determined to have contributed to a discrepancy. Also as discussed, please ensure the method of denaturation is consistently documented on the record for controlled drug destruction activities moving forward.

1. Review the following reference regarding emergency anaphylaxis kits and written protocols for managing these situations. Note that the recommended epinephrine dose for individuals weighing 45kg or more is 0.5mg. Ensure you obtain and stock sufficient products to manage all dosing requirements of your patient (and your updated your policies to reflect this recommendation). Post the dose administration guide in your counseling room for easy reference. https://www.canada.ca/en/public-health/services/publications/healthy-living/canadian- immunization-guide-part-2-vaccine-safety/page-4-early-vaccine-reactions-including- anaphylaxis.html 2. Ensure your needlestick injury protocol is readily available in your private counselling rooms. See here for a printable algorithm and other materials: https://scp.in1touch.org/document/3633/ REF_Needlestick_Injury.pdf Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

At the time of this assessment, you did not have any active patients receiving opioid agonist therapy at your pharmacy. We will continue to review the status of such services at upcoming assessments to ensure compliance with relevant standards and guidelines. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

We discussed Standards of Practice related to informed consent for physical contact with patients when administering certain injections (e.g. ventrogluteal, abdominal, etc). You indicated that pharmacists are occasionally administering abdominal injections at your pharmacy, so the following consent principles are important to review and discuss with your patients: - evidence that the patient has given informed consent about the nature, purpose, and duration of the contact required for this injection; - evidence that the patient is offered appropriate privacy for the physical contact that occurs; - evidence that the patient has given informed consent as to the room within which the regulated member will be providing the professional service that involves physical contact; - evidence that the patient is offered a to have a chaperon present during the contact. Share these requirements with your team and ensure they are being utilized in such situations moving forward.

In reviewing some of the prescription adaptations completed by pharmacists today, it was noted that aspects of the patient's chief complaint (or history of present illness) was not always documented on the record. For instance, one example involved the dose adaptation of a patient's azithromycin for community acquired pneumonia. However, the pharmacist's assessment of this indication, inclusive of the patient's symptoms and treatment history, was not recorded in the documentation note. We discussed the expectation that pharmacists document their own thorough assessment with each prescription adaptation or extension. Use the Chat Check Chart adaptation tool to ensure your documentation includes at a minimum: - history of present illness including timelines of symptoms and locations of symptoms - previous use of medications including dates, timelines, and outcomes - relevant Netcare data, including labwork (indicate what information on Netcare was consulted) - differential diagnosis considerations - alternatives considered by the pharmacist Have a few examples from each pharmacist to discuss at the next visit. https://abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/files/PrescriptionAdaptationGuide.pdf Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

We discussed new standards pertaining to equity, diversity, inclusion (EDI); accessibility; cultural sensitivity, and Indigenous cultural safety (SPPPT domain 1). Each pharmacy needs to have standard operating procedures (SOPs) to support the pharmacy team in incorporating these concepts into practice. We discussed some patient groups who may be subject to barriers to care due to prejudice, physical or cognitive barriers, etc. Some key considerations for you to consider include: -Changes that can be made in the pharmacy to be more inclusive for these groups -Changes to make the space more welcoming (e.g., language, pharmacy appearance, physical environment) -Staff training and ongoing monitoring to ensure implementation of these changes. https://abpharmacy.ca/news/treating-people-with-respect-and-dignity/ Encourage team members to review the article in the Link above and begin to reflect on your current approaches for meeting EDI, accessibility, cultural sensitivity and Indigenous cultural safety expectations in your pharmacy.

We reviewed several care plans where general goals of therapy were documented on the record. For instance, a common goal of therapy documented for patients taking anti-hypertensives was "BP < 140/90 or BP <130/80 for Diabetes." We reviewed the importance of clarifying and documenting mutual goals of therapy with patients to ensure therapeutic assessment and monitoring activities are patient-specific. Considering this, ensure to collect and document the patient's specific goal(s) of therapy on the record beginning with your next care plan (or other care intervention such as initial access prescription, adaptation, etc.). Use these goals to guide your assessment, care planning, and follow up process and set aside a few examples demonstrating this activity for us to review together at the next visit. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

category.raw: Pharmacy Practice : Monitoring and Follow- up

findings.description_verbatim: We reviewed several prescribing examples (initial access, adaptation, extension) where follow up planning was somewhat general and unclear. For instance, one example involved a pharmacist prescribing valacyclovir for a patient where it was indicated that the patient "is aware to follow-up with the pharmacy with questions." However, we discussed the expectation that pharmacists develop and implement monitoring and follow up plans with patients for whom they've prescribed, and that delegating this activity to the patient is not always appropriate. With this in mind, discuss with your team the requirement that a documented and patient-specific monitoring plan is included within the summary note for prescribing interventions. The monitoring plan should identify who is following up, when this is expected to occur, and what parameters are being assessed at that time. Further, aim to use the Kroll follow up system more consistently to keep track of follow up activities across all pharmacists. Set aside some examples of follow up care completed by your pharmacists using this system for us to review at the next assessment. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

As per Standard 5.5.1 (https://abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/files/ACP_SOLP.pdf) A licensee must ensure the following with respect to digital records: a) ensure all data required under the legislative framework is backed up at least once daily; b) store a copy of the backup data a. at an approved off-site location, b. using a secure cloud-based service provider that stores data within Canada, c. in a fire- and theft-resistant safe; c) ensure the backup is stored so that it is retrievable in the event the system malfunctions or is destroyed; d) ensure the backup is kept securely to avoid theft or unauthorized access, use, or disclosure; and e) ensure there is a disaster recovery plan in place as part of the policies and procedures of the pharmacy to restore patient data and systems as quickly as possible in the event of a data loss or system failure At the time of the assessment, it was not clear how your pharmacy complied with these requirements. As such, within the next 30 days, take steps to confirm the processes in place for backing up required information in your pharmacy and document these policies for the awareness of all staff.

For care plans, ensure your documentation includes evidence that a CHECK (Using the Chat, Check, Chart model) assessment has been performed for all medications on the best possible medication history list. Ensure that you establish a follow-up plan with patient specific monitoring parameters for each identified drug therapy problem and medical condition (even if the full work up will occur at a subsequent visit). Ensure you document when follow-up will occur and who will complete it. Aim to have one new care plan or care plan follow-up to discuss at the next visit. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

We discussed that the standards of practice and legislative framework do not permit pharmacies to act as a "depot" for prescriptions to be delivered to. We discussed that mail order pharmacies must deliver prescriptions to patients directly, and not through other pharmacies that are located closer to your patients. You indicated that your site has been asked by certain mail order pharmacies to receive and store refrigerated medications in cases where they cannot deliver directly to the patient. Prior to my next assessment, make contact with these pharmacies to advise them that this practice must cease, and they must either deliver drugs directly to the patient, or transfer the prescription to the patient's nearest pharmacy.

category.raw: Laboratory and Point- of-Care Testing : Point- of-Care Testing Program

findings.description_verbatim: Within the next 30 days, please update your policies to better define your strep testing procedures. Specifically, outline a step-by-step guide that addresses the following: • Screening and assessment activities • Calibration of equipment (instrument/reagent) as required by the manufacturer. • Training requirements and procedures. I would suggest that training should also include a review of the device user manual(s). • Personal protective equipment (PPE). Your SOP should include information regarding mask usage. • Establishment of acceptable target or reference ranges for QC materials. For example, control testing instructions. We will review this again together at the next assessment.
Beginning with the next narcotic count, ensure an audit trail is recorded indicating any personnel involved in the count, as well as the pharmacist who oversaw the activity.

Review the following resources regarding the handling and administration recommendations for hazardous drug injections in the pharmacy. - NIOSH Reference: https://www.cdc.gov/niosh/docs/2016-161/pdfs/2016-161.pdf?id=10.26616/ NIOSHPUB2016161 (see Table 5 on Page 32 for specific recommendations) - ACP Guidelines on Injection Safety: https://abpharmacy.ca/sites/default/files/ Guidelines_Injection_Safety.pdf (see Section 6 for specific guidance) Review your own procedures for hazardous injectables to ensure the following considerations are in place: - Hazardous drugs are stored separately from other medications if possible. - Employees and others at risk from handling hazardous drugs are identified and provided with adequate training and equipment - Cytotoxic spill kits are available and staff are trained in spill management - Adequate personal protective equipment including but not limited to gloves and facial protection is worn for administration of hazardous drugs. Examples of gloves are here: https:// www.medisca.ca/products/nitrile-gloves-9-4mil - Additional personal protective equipment is used where there is potential for splash, spill or aerosolization. Personal protective equipment is disposable where possible. - Hazardous drug waste and equipment is disposed at point‐of‐use into a cytotoxic waste container with minimal manipulation (e.g., needles and syringes are left intact). Document and implement your own procedures for managing and administering these products by injection in your pharmacy. Aim to have this completed by the next assessment.

With respect to homeopathic products, please implement a plan regarding the standard that requires drugs to be displayed and advertised independently of such products. This can be accomplished by means of physical separation or signage such that a member of the public can easily distinguish drugs in the pharmacy from any homeopathic products.

We also discussed the use of your "paperless" Kroll System, whereby the processing of a prescription is broken down into several steps and several people are involved in the filling of the prescription. Components of the audit trail for dispensed prescriptions are recorded electronically in Kroll with no accompanying written record or written/electronic signature of the individual involved in filling or packaging tasks. This system relies on adherence to policies and procedures to maintain the accuracy of the audit trail including the following features: - ensuring there is an “auto lock” or "auto log out” feature for workstations that have been inactive for a period of time - ensuring your policies indicate that staff log out of their workstations when moving to perform other tasks - the policy that staff attempt to use the same workstations and avoid sharing terminals as much as possible. Continue to analyze and reassess your system and procedures to ensure an accurate and complete audit trail is being maintained for all prescriptions dispensed by your pharmacy.

We discussed the requirement to develop and document a plan to ensure continuity of patient care in the event planned or unplanned pharmacy closures and the loss or loss of access to pharmacy records. At the time of this assessment, you weren't sure if corporate policies were available that would provide guidance to your pharmacy team during a disaster. Prior to my next assessment, please contact the appropriate party to determine if such policies are in place. We will review progress on this item at the next assessment. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

We discussed new standards pertaining to continuous quality improvement and the importance of supporting safe patient care through the creation of a safety culture. We further discussed the requirements to comply with the college’s continuous quality improvement program (CQI+). As discussed, all requirements must be in place by February 10, 2026. I have included relevant links below to support our discussion at the next visit. Please ensure to review relevant links to support your implementation of the program. Please note that policies and procedures should be updated to support continuous quality improvement for the licensed pharmacy. Link Article: https://abpharmacy.ca/news/acp-launches-cqi/ Q&A: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/CQI_QA.pdf Guide to CQI+: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/CQI_Guide.pdf CQI+ Quick-start Checklist: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/CQI_Checklist.pdf CQI+ Program Requirement: https://abpharmacy.ca/regulated-members/cqi/overview/program- requirements/ CQI+ Platform Requirement: https://abpharmacy.ca/regulated-members/cqi/overview/platform- requirements/ Mandatory Reporting to the NIDR: https://abpharmacy.ca/regulated-members/cqi/overview/ mandatory-reporting-to-the-nidr/ Relevant Resources: https://abpharmacy.ca/regulated-members/cqi/resources/

I noted some return-to-stock medications lacked all required labelling information (i.e. adequate drug identification, lot and expiry dates). You've recently installed a new Kroll operating system, which allows staff to record lot numbers and expiry dates within the transaction. Moving forward, ensure that all medications that are returned to stock are appropriately labelled with this information. Additionally, review your existing policies and procedures for tracking lot numbers and expiry dates to ensure this information is documented on the record moving forward. Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date

category.raw: Non-sterile Compounding : Training and Skills Assessment

findings.description_verbatim: In order to maintain requirements to perform Level A compounding, you must administer and maintain a training and skills assessment program. There are two distinct components of training requirements for non-sterile compounding: theoretical training and skills assessments. The training component of the program covers knowledge-related aspects of non-sterile compounding as well as the policies and procedures in place at the pharmacy. The skills assessment evaluates whether compounding personnel apply their knowledge correctly and prepare products in compliance with the Standards. Prior to my next assessment, please review Table 1 in Section 5.2 of the nonsterile compounding standards at the following link to compare your existing training and skills assessment program with the all required items. This procedure must include a tracking sheet or record for all personnel that have completed the assessment and must also be repeated annually. We will review progress on both items at the next assessment. https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/Guidance_Non-sterile_Compounding.pdf

In order to maintain foundational expectations for Level A nonsterile compounding, you must have a process for conducting and documenting master formulation records. A master formulation record must be completed for each compounded product to ensure an appropriate process and equipment is followed to prepare the medication. Master Formulation Records for compounded preparations must be reviewed and updated at least annually as well. With this in mind, please develop and document your procedure for updating your master formulation records moving forward. Aim to have this completed and implemented for two of your more common compounded preparations within the next 30 days. As discussed, the key considerations to include in the updated formulation record are: - a reputable formula to base your process on - the required equipment for the preparation - the required active ingredients for the preparation - the process staff are to follow for preparing the compound An example formulation record is available here: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/ Compounding_TemplateMasterFormulationRecord.pdf

In order to maintain requirements to perform Level A compounding, you must maintain policies and procedures describing your site-specific preparation processes. The policies should include: - the types/level of compounding that will occur in your pharmacy - the names and contact information of your contracted compounding and repackaging pharmacies (including ordering procedures) - the process for compound entry in the patient file (including audit trail tracking details using the compound lot or prescription number) - the process for accessing SDS information for APIs - the process for completing and documenting risk assessments - the process for completing and documenting master formulation records Aim to begin documenting your procedures prior to my next assessment in 6 weeks.

In order to maintain foundational expectations for Level A nonsterile compounding, you must have a process for conducting and documenting risk assessments. A risk assessment must be completed for each compounded product to ensure appropriate equipment, clothing, and processes are followed for preparing the medication. Risk assessments for compounded preparations must be reviewed and updated at least annually as well. With this in mind, please develop and document your procedure for updating your risk assessments moving forward. Aim to have this completed and implemented for two of your more common compounded preparations within the next 30 days. As discussed, the key considerations to include in the updated risk assessment are: - assessment of the frequency of preparing the compound - assessment of the quantities prepared for each compound - assessment of the concentrations of ingredients used in the compound - assessment of the cumulative risk of exposure for the ingredients in the compound These factors can be considered in comparison to the previous risk assessment documented for each compound to determine if changes to your practice or infrastructure are needed. Ensure the risk assessment is documented to include a revision date and audit trail of the compounding personnel who completed the review. The following link includes an example of a risk assessment document: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp- content/uploads/Compounding_Risk_assessment_2.pdf

As discussed, the licensee must ensure drug error documentation is reviewed at least quarterly to evaluate whether practice changes or preventive measures are required to prevent future drug errors, and to assess whether any changes implemented as a result of a drug error were successful in advancing patient safety. In the absence of medication errors, begin documenting close calls and incorporating this data into your quarterly review. When assessing close calls or near misses, aim to identify key behaviors that prevented the event from becoming an error. For instance, if showing a patient their medication at pick up resulted in the identification of a near miss, note this key activity in your quarterly review summary The quarterly review must be documented and reviewed with all staff. Use the following resources to complete your quarterly review of drug errors for the first quarter of 2025. We will review this together at the next assessment. 1. Quarterly review report template: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/ DrugIncidentQuarterlyReview_Fillable.pdf 2. Sample of completed quarterly review report: https://abpharmacy.ca/wp-content/uploads/ DrugIncidentQuarterlyReview_Sample.pdf Date State Category Due Date Person Responsible Completed Date
Please complete and document the training and skills assessments for relevant nonsterile compounding personnel (likely yourself and Sean) prior to my next assessment.

You and your team have made great progress at implementing the practice goals we set at the previous assessment. Continue to focus on the following areas to maintain momentum and to ensure practices are applied consistently: - indications are consistently being confirmed at intake and documented on the record - ensuring a 'check' assessment is performed for all medications on the patient's BPMH - patient-specific details (such as onset of symptoms, aggravating/remitting factors, etc) are documented on the record for prescribing decisions - Netcare information is consulted and documented on the record for relevant clinical decisions - collaborative notes regarding your interventions for patients get consistently shared with relevant health care providers - a reference or guidelines is used to support initial prescribing decisions - a clear and patient-specific monitoring and follow up plan is documented with each assessment summary - assessment information related to ongoing injections (B12) is routinely documented on the record of care - patient consent is clearly documented on the record for virtual care activities Please set aside a few will review a few more patient care activities (including APA cases) at the next assessment.
